transcript.presenting_concern: Acute suicidal ideation following breakup — safety planning

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Taylor, thank you for coming in today. I know this was an emergency appointment. Your mom called us this morning concerned about your safety. Before we start, I just want to say — whatever's going on, whatever you're feeling, you're in the right place. This is what we're here for. Can you tell me what's been happening?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet, looking at floor] I don't know. Everything just got to be too much. I broke up with Jamie — well, Jamie broke up with me. Three days ago. And it's like the world ended. I know that sounds dramatic but I literally cannot see the point of anything anymore.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That doesn't sound dramatic to me at all. Breakups at any age can feel devastating, and from what I'm hearing, this wasn't just a relationship — Jamie was really important to you. It sounds like you're in a lot of pain right now.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [nods, eyes watering] Yeah. A lot of pain.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Taylor, I need to ask you some direct questions, and I want you to know that whatever your answers are, this is a safe space. I'm here to help, not to judge. Nothing you say is going to shock me or make me think less of you. These are questions I ask because I care about your safety. Okay?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Are you having thoughts of suicide?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause, picking at sleeve] Yeah. Since yesterday.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I appreciate your honesty. That takes courage to say out loud. Can you tell me more about those thoughts? What form do they take?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like... I keep thinking about what if I just wasn't here anymore. If I just disappeared. At first it was vague — like wishing I could go to sleep and not wake up. That started maybe Saturday. But yesterday it got... more specific. I started thinking about how I would actually do it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thank you for telling me that. I want to be direct with you because that's what helps in moments like this. When you say more specific — have you thought about a particular method?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My mom has sleeping pills. In the medicine cabinet upstairs. I know where they are. I thought about taking all of them.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Have you taken any steps toward doing that? Have you held the pills, counted them, moved them to your room, looked up information about them?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Well — I opened the medicine cabinet last night and looked at them. I picked up the bottle and read the label. But I didn't take them out or anything. I put it back.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. That distinction matters, Taylor. Looking is different from acting. Picking up the bottle and putting it back — that tells me something important. Something in you said "no." What stopped you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My dog. Penny. She was sleeping on my bed and when I came back from the bathroom she lifted her head and looked at me with those eyes and I just... I thought "who would take care of her?" Nobody loves her like I do. My mom would try but she works all the time. And then I thought about my mom finding me and I couldn't — [voice breaking] — I couldn't do that to her. She's already been through so much with the divorce and everything.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [gentle pause] Those connections — to Penny, to your mom — those are what we call protective factors. They are real and they matter enormously. Even in the darkest moment last night, something in you said "not yet, not now." I want to work with that part of you today. That part is strong, Taylor, even if it doesn't feel like it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It doesn't feel strong. It feels like I'm barely hanging on.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And barely hanging on is still hanging on. You're here. You let your mom bring you in. That's an act of survival. Can I ask — have you had thoughts like this before? Before this week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Not really. I mean, in high school I went through a rough patch sophomore year when my parents were divorcing. I felt really low then. But I don't think I ever thought about actually... doing something. This is different. This feels darker.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Different how?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like before it was just sadness. This feels like — like there's a weight pressing on my chest and I can't breathe and I can't see any way it gets better. Before, even when things were bad, I could imagine a future. Right now I can't imagine tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That inability to imagine a future — that hopelessness — it's one of the hallmark symptoms of severe depression. I want you to hear something important: that feeling is not reality. It feels absolutely real, I know it does, but it's your brain in a crisis state narrowing your vision so you can only see pain. It's like looking through a tunnel — the tunnel is real, but what's outside the tunnel is also real. You just can't see it right now.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [wiping eyes] I want to believe that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You don't have to fully believe it yet. You just have to be willing to entertain the possibility. Can you do that? Can you hold space for "maybe this won't last forever, even though it feels like it will"?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Yeah. Maybe. I mean, logically I know depression is a thing. I know it's treatable. It just doesn't FEEL like that from the inside.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's exactly right. And that's why we're here — because when you're inside the tunnel, you need someone outside it to remind you there's more. Now, I feel stupid. It's just a breakup. People break up all the time. Why am I this... broken about it?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I feel stupid. It's just a breakup. People break up all the time. Why am I this... broken about it?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let me challenge that thought. A breakup isn't "just" anything. It's losing a relationship, yes, but it's also losing a future you imagined, a daily source of comfort and validation, a piece of your identity. And here's what I'm hearing from you — the breakup didn't happen in isolation. It sounds like things were already hard before Jamie ended things. Can you tell me about that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Honestly? Not great. I've been struggling since starting college last fall. I left all my high school friends — they all went to different schools or stayed local and I went two hours away. My roommate and I don't click at all. She's nice enough but we have nothing in common and she's always out with her own friends. My grades are terrible because I can't concentrate. I stopped going to my 8 AM because I couldn't get out of bed. Jamie was basically the one good thing. The one person who made me feel like I belonged somewhere. Like someone chose me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the breakup didn't create your depression — it removed your primary coping resource. The low mood, isolation, difficulty concentrating, trouble getting out of bed — those were already there, building since fall. Jamie was the anchor, and now that anchor is gone and you're adrift.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. That's exactly it. I'm adrift. Like there's nothing tethering me to anything.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I hear that. And I want to acknowledge how painful that is. Being nineteen, away from your support system, struggling academically, feeling disconnected — and then losing the one relationship that felt solid. That's not a small thing. Your pain is proportional to what you're experiencing.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nobody else seems to think so. My roommate was like "you'll find someone else" and my dad said "you're too young to be this upset about a boyfriend." Like, thanks dad.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Those responses — while well-intentioned — are invalidating. They minimize your experience. And I imagine hearing them made you feel more alone, not less.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Like nobody gets it. Nobody sees how bad it really is.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Well, I see it. And I want you to know that what you're describing — the concentration problems, the sleep issues, the withdrawal, the hopelessness, the loss of interest in things that used to matter — this is textbook major depression. This isn't weakness. This isn't being overdramatic. This is an illness, the same way pneumonia is an illness. And like pneumonia, it's treatable. You wouldn't tell someone with pneumonia to just "get over it," right?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. That would be dumb.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. So I need you to extend yourself the same compassion you'd extend to someone else. Depression is a medical condition involving neurotransmitter imbalances — serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine. Your brain chemistry is not functioning optimally right now, and that's affecting everything — your mood, your cognition, your motivation, your sleep, your ability to see hope. The good news? We have effective treatments. Therapy. Medication. Lifestyle interventions. People recover from this. Every single day.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [nods slowly] Okay.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Now, Taylor, here's what I want to do. I want us to work together on getting you through this acute crisis — this immediate danger — and then we're going to address the bigger picture: the depression, the isolation at school, building support. Are you willing to work on a safety plan with me right now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. I don't want to die. I just want the pain to stop.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I hear you. And I believe you. Those are actually two very different things — wanting to die and wanting the pain to stop. Suicidal thoughts are often not about death at all. They're about escape. Your brain is looking for the exit from unbearable suffering. Our job is to find other exits — ones that don't end your life. Ones that actually address the pain.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. Yeah. I want that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. So a safety plan is like an emergency protocol — it's a step-by-step guide for what to do when those dark thoughts come. We're going to build it together and you're going to keep it somewhere accessible. I've got a form here. Let's fill it out. Step one is about reducing access to means. The sleeping pills your mom has — I'd like her to lock them up or remove them from the house entirely. Would you be okay with me talking to her about that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. That's fine. I don't... I don't want the temptation there. Last night was too easy. The fact that I could just... reach out and grab them. That scared me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It's good that it scared you. That fear is healthy — it's your survival instinct saying "this is dangerous." And you're right that access matters. Research shows that putting even a small barrier between a person and lethal means can save lives, because suicidal crises are often time-limited. The intense urge passes, usually within minutes to hours. If you can get through that window, you survive. Is there anything else at home that concerns you? Firearms, sharps, other medications?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. No guns. My mom doesn't keep anything like that. Just the pills.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What about at school? In your dorm?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Nothing there either. I mean, I have Tylenol but that's it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. I'd actually recommend removing the Tylenol too, or giving it to your RA to hold for you. Acetaminophen in large quantities can cause serious liver damage. You can always ask for it back if you need a headache pill, but having a bottle of anything accessible during a crisis isn't ideal.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I can do that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Step two is recognizing your warning signs — the thoughts, feelings, or situations that tell you you're moving from "feeling terrible" to "actively thinking about suicide." What are those for you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Um. When I start thinking "nobody would miss me" or "things will never get better." When everything feels permanent — like this is forever and nothing can change. Also late at night, alone in my room, when it's dark and quiet. That's when the thoughts get loudest.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Nighttime is a very common trigger. There's less distraction, less connection, and your defenses are down from fatigue. So your warning signs are: the "nobody would miss me" thought, the "things will never get better" thought, and being alone at night when things feel permanent. Let me write those down. [writing] Good. When you notice any of these, that's your cue to move to step three.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Step three is internal coping strategies — things you can do on your own, by yourself, to ride out the wave of suicidal thoughts before you need to call someone. What helps you even a little bit when you're in that dark space?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Walking my dog. Penny. She makes me get outside even when I don't want to. She just looks at me and I can't say no to her. And music — putting on headphones and lying in the dark with music kind of... insulates me from the thoughts for a while. Like a cocoon.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Dog walk and music. What else? Think about anything that shifts your attention, even temporarily. Some people find ice cubes helpful — holding them, the shock of cold. Some people take showers. Some people do something physical.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Hot showers help. I stand in the shower and just let the water hit me and it's like... it grounds me? Like I'm in my body again instead of in my head.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a grounding technique. Perfect. So your internal coping list is: walk Penny, music with headphones, and hot shower. Three options. Now, what about distraction? Is there anything you can watch, read, play that absorbs your attention?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I used to play this stupid phone game — Stardew Valley. It's just farming and it's repetitive but it's calming. I haven't played in months because nothing felt enjoyable but maybe I could force myself.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Sometimes with depression, you have to do the activity first and the enjoyment follows, rather than waiting to feel motivated. So yes — add Stardew Valley to the list. Even if it doesn't feel good, it occupies your hands and your brain. That's the goal during a crisis: survive the minutes until the intensity passes.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. Walk Penny, music, shower, game.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Four options. Good. Step four: people you can reach out to when the internal strategies aren't enough. Who in your life can you call or text when you're in crisis?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My mom. Obviously. She'd drop everything. My friend Sarah from high school — she always picks up, even at like 2 AM. We have that kind of friendship. And maybe my cousin Diego. We're close. He went through depression himself a few years ago so he gets it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Three people. That's a strong list. I want you to put their numbers on this card in order. Do you feel comfortable telling any of them what's going on — that you've been having suicidal thoughts?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My mom knows. She's the one who brought me here. Sarah... I don't know. I don't want to burden her.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I hear the concern about being a burden. Let me ask you this — if Sarah called you at 2 AM and said "Taylor, I'm having a really dark night and I need to talk," would you feel burdened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. I'd be glad she called me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. People who love us want to be there for us. The "I'm a burden" thought is another depression distortion — it's not reality. You don't have to tell Sarah everything, but you could say "hey, I'm going through a rough time, can we talk?" That's enough. Would you be willing to reach out to her this week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. I think she'd want to know.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Step five: professional resources. That's me — you can call our office anytime during business hours and they'll get a message to me. We also have an after-hours crisis line. And there's the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline — you call or text 988, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, free and confidential. There's also the Crisis Text Line — you text HOME to 741741. Do you have 988 in your phone?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's add it right now. Pull out your phone and save both — 988 as "Crisis Lifeline" and 741741 as "Crisis Text." Sometimes when you're in the moment, texting feels easier than calling.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [typing] Okay. Done. Both saved.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Step six is about making your environment safe — we've covered the pills and the Tylenol. The last piece is what Reason for Living can you write at the bottom of this card? One thing that matters to you, that reminds you why you're choosing to stay.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Penny. And my mom. I can't do that to my mom.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Write that down in your own words. However it resonates.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [writing] "Penny needs me. Mom can't lose me." [pause] Is that morbid?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Not at all. It's true. It's YOUR truth. And in a crisis moment, reading those words might be the thing that gets you to pick up the phone instead of picking up the pills. That's what this card is for.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [holding the card, looking at it] This feels... concrete. Like a plan instead of just drowning.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's exactly what it is. When you're in crisis, your brain goes into tunnel vision and you can't think clearly. The safety plan does the thinking for you — it's already laid out, step by step. All you have to do is follow it. Now, I want to talk about treatment going forward. You mentioned you've been depressed since fall but never received treatment. Is that right?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Right. I thought it would pass. Like it did in high school.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Sometimes depression does resolve on its own, but when it's been persistent for months and is now this severe — with suicidal ideation, functional impairment, sleep disruption — it's unlikely to just go away without intervention. I want to recommend a two-pronged approach: therapy with me, twice a week to start, and medication.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Medication... like antidepressants?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Yes. Specifically, I'd like to start you on fluoxetine — that's the generic for Prozac. It's an SSRI, which means it helps your brain use serotonin more effectively. It's well-studied in your age group, it's safe, and it's effective. The catch is that it takes two to four weeks to reach full effect, so it's not an instant fix.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Are there side effects?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Common ones include mild nausea the first few days, maybe some headache, occasionally increased anxiety initially. Most people find side effects are mild and resolve within a week or two. I also want to be transparent — in people under 25, there's a boxed warning about antidepressants potentially increasing suicidal thoughts early in treatment. The actual risk is very small, but it means we monitor you closely for the first few weeks. That's another reason for twice-weekly sessions.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's kind of scary — a medication for depression could make suicidal thoughts worse?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I understand that's concerning. The theory is that the medication sometimes restores energy and motivation before it lifts mood — so someone who was too depressed to act on thoughts might briefly have more energy while still feeling hopeless. That's rare, but it's why we watch carefully. The flip side is that untreated depression is far more dangerous than treated depression. The risk of NOT treating is much higher than the risk of treating. Does that make sense?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. I'd rather try something than keep feeling like this.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. I'm also going to prescribe a short-term sleep aid — trazodone, which is non-lethal in overdose and also has mild antidepressant effects. I suspect you're not sleeping well?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I haven't slept more than four hours a night since the breakup. I just lie there staring at the ceiling.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Sleep deprivation makes everything worse — mood, concentration, impulse control, pain tolerance. Getting your sleep stabilized is actually a safety intervention in itself. Trazodone will help you sleep, and your mom will hold it and give you one pill at a time, not the whole bottle. Sound okay?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. That's smart. One at a time.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Now, let me ask about some other things. Have you been eating?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Not really. Maybe one meal a day. Nothing sounds good.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Are you drinking alcohol or using any substances?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. I don't drink. I smoked weed a few times at school but not since this started.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. I'd recommend staying away from any substances right now — even weed can worsen depression and impair judgment during a crisis. What about self-harm? Cutting, burning, anything like that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Never.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Have you given away any possessions, written any notes, said any goodbyes?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Nothing like that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Those are all good signs. Your risk factors are the suicidal ideation with a specific plan and access, the severe depression, the isolation, and the recent major loss. Your protective factors are your connection to your mom and dog, no history of attempts, no substance use, willingness to seek help, and your genuine ambivalence — you don't want to die, you want the pain to stop. That ambivalence is protective.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So am I... am I going to be hospitalized? Is that what this is?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a fair question. Based on our conversation — you have no intent to act right now, you have protective factors, you're engaging with safety planning, and you have a supportive home environment — I don't believe hospitalization is necessary today. But I want to be clear: if at any point between now and our next session, you feel like you can't keep yourself safe, you go to the ER or you call 988. No shame in that. Sometimes people need that higher level of care and that's okay. How does that feel to you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Relieved. I don't want to go to a hospital. I want to go home with my dog and my mom and try to sleep.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's what we're going to set up. Now — for right now, today, tonight — can you commit to using the safety plan if those red flag thoughts come up? Walk Penny, put on music, take a shower, play the game. If that doesn't help: call Sarah, call your mom, call Diego. If THAT doesn't help: call 988 or come to the ER.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. I can do that. I promise.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. I'm going to ask you something else. Can you tell me — on a scale of 1 to 10, right now, where is your intent to act on suicidal thoughts?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Right now? Like a 2. Talking about it made it feel less overwhelming. In here I feel safer. Like someone gets it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That makes sense. Connection reduces the intensity — isolation amplifies it. That's actually one of the key things we're going to work on in therapy: building more connection into your life so you're not relying on a single relationship for all of your emotional support. Right now, your support network is thin. We're going to thicken it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'd like that. I've been so lonely.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I hear that. And loneliness is painful in its own right — it's not just an inconvenience, it actually affects brain chemistry and health. You deserve connection. You're worthy of it. The depression tells you otherwise, but the depression is lying.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [small laugh] The depression is lying. I like that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It's a good mantra. Now, I want to talk about one more thing before we wrap up. School. You mentioned grades are suffering, you're not going to classes. Is it possible you need to take a medical leave this semester?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I've thought about that. But then I'd feel like even more of a failure. Everyone else can handle college. Why can't I?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Comparison is another depression trap. You don't know what's happening behind other people's closed doors. But more importantly — taking a medical leave isn't failure. It's wisdom. If you had mono, you'd take a medical leave without shame. Depression is no different. Getting well and going back strong is better than pushing through and failing classes, which then becomes another source of shame. Just something to think about. We don't have to decide today.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. I'll think about it. My mom mentioned it too.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. For now, our priorities are: one, keep you safe tonight and this week. Two, start medication. Three, get your sleep back. Four, see you again Thursday. Everything else — school, social connections, longer-term therapy — we'll address as you stabilize.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thursday. I can make Thursday.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I'm going to bring your mom in now to discuss the means restriction — the pills and the medication management plan. I'll give you both a copy of the safety plan. I also want to give you something — [opens desk drawer] — this is a grounding stone. It's just a smooth rock. When the thoughts get loud, hold it in your hand and squeeze it. Feel its weight, its temperature. It keeps you in your body when your mind is spiraling. Silly-looking, but it works.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [takes stone] It's heavy. That's... actually kind of nice.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Keep it in your pocket or on your nightstand. Think of it as your "pause button." Now, before I bring your mom in — is there anything else you want to tell me that you don't want her to hear?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Um. [pause] I didn't tell her about looking at the pills last night. I just told her I was having thoughts. She doesn't know I went to the cabinet.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Would you be comfortable with me sharing that with her, in terms that help her understand why locking up the pills is urgent? I won't dramatize it, but I want her to take the means restriction seriously.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. She should know. I don't want to keep secrets that could get me hurt.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's brave. That's you choosing safety over avoidance. I'm proud of you for that. One last thing — I want you to know that what happened today, sitting here, telling a stranger your darkest thoughts — that is one of the hardest things a person can do. You did it. You showed up. And that tells me you have fight in you, even if you can't feel it right now.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [tears] Thank you, Dr. Reeves. I didn't think talking would help but... I feel a little less like I'm drowning. Like someone threw me a rope, at least.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's what therapy is — a rope. You still have to hold on, but you're not alone in the water anymore. I'll see you Thursday, same time. And remember — the safety plan lives in your pocket. Use it before you need it, not after.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I will. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let me get your mom.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Mrs. Morrison, thank you for bringing Taylor in today. That was exactly the right call. Taylor and I have had a really productive conversation, and I want to share some things with you — Taylor has given permission for this. First, the immediate concern: Taylor has been having suicidal thoughts and last night opened the medicine cabinet where your sleeping pills are. Taylor didn't take any action beyond looking, but the proximity of lethal means during a crisis is a risk factor we need to eliminate.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Oh god. Taylor, honey—

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'm okay, Mom. I'm okay right now.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Taylor IS okay right now. But what I need from you is to remove those pills from accessible reach today. Lock them in your car, give them to a neighbor to hold, or bring them here — we can dispose of them. This is the single most important safety step you can take tonight.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'll get rid of them. I'll flush them when we get home. I didn't even think — I'm so sorry, Taylor.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: There's no blame here. You couldn't have known. You're doing the right thing now. I'm also prescribing Taylor two medications — fluoxetine for the depression and trazodone for sleep. I need you to hold both medications and dispense them one dose at a time. Can you do that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Absolutely. Whatever it takes.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I'm also giving you both a copy of Taylor's safety plan. Mrs. Morrison, your job is to be available if Taylor needs to use it — to pick up the phone at any hour, to not judge, to just be present. Can you do that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Of course. Always. Taylor knows that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. We're going to meet again Thursday. Between now and then, your household should be pill-free, Taylor should be trying to sleep with the trazodone, and if anything escalates — any concern that Taylor can't stay safe — you go to the emergency department. Understood?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Understood. Thank you, Dr. Reeves.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're both doing the right thing. Taylor — you're not broken. You're in pain, and pain is treatable. I'll see you Thursday.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thursday. We'll be here.

patient.name: Taylor Morrison
diagnoses: Major Depressive Disorder, Single Episode, Severe (F32.2)

goals: Acute suicidal ideation following breakup — safety planning
